generations.code:
If degree = 1: We should command a direction that's NOT the free neighbor. Then the robot must move to a direction ≠ our command and not blocked. The only free direction is the free neighbor, which is ≠ our command. So the robot MUST move to the free neighbor!

So: if a cell has exactly 1 free neighbor, we can force the robot to move there (by commanding any other direction). If that neighbor is also forceable (or is the lab), then this cell is forceable.

More generally: a cell is forceable if it has exactly 1 free neighbor that is forceable (or is the lab). Because we can command any direction except toward that forceable neighbor, forcing the robot to go there.

Wait, what if it has more free neighbors but all except one are forceable? No - the robot gets to CHOOSE which non-commanded direction to go. So if we command direction X, the robot picks any direction ≠ X that's free. We need ALL such choices to lead to forceable cells... but we get to pick what command to send.

So: cell C is forceable if there exists a command direction d such that ALL free neighbors of C except possibly the one in direction d are forceable (or lab). And there's at least one free neighbor not in direction d (otherwise robot does nothing).

Actually: we send command d. Robot moves to any free neighbor except in direction d. For C to be forceable, all free neighbors except the one in direction d must be forceable/lab. Also, the robot must have at least one valid move (otherwise it does nothing, which is bad unless C = lab).

So C is forceable if: there exists a direction d such that:
1. All free neighbors of C in directions ≠ d are forceable/lab
2. At least one free neighbor of C is in a direction ≠ d (so robot actually moves)

This means: the number of free neighbors that are NOT forceable/lab must be 0 when we exclude direction d. So at most 1 free neighbor is not forceable/lab (the one in direction d). And there must be at least one free neighbor ≠ d.

So C is forceable if: (number of non-forceable free neighbors) ≤ 1, AND (total free neighbors - 1) ≥ 1, i.e., total free neighbors ≥ 2... wait no.

Let me restate: C is forceable if there exists d such that every free neighbor except possibly direction-d is forceable/lab, AND at least one free neighbor exists in a direction ≠ d.

This is a BFS/propagation: start with lab as forceable. Then propagate: a cell becomes forceable when the count of non-forceable free neighbors ≤ 1 AND we can pick d to exclude the one bad neighbor, AND total_free_neighbors - (1 if bad_neighbor_in_d else 0) ≥ 1.

Let me implement: for each cell, track `bad` = number of free neighbors not yet forceable. Cell becomes forceable when `bad ≤ 1` and `(free_degree - (1 if bad==1 else 0)) ≥ 1`, i.e., free_degree ≥ 2 when bad==1, or free_degree ≥ 1 when bad==0.

Simplified: forceable when (bad == 0 and free_degree >= 1) or (bad == 1 and free_degree >= 2).

Use BFS starting from lab.
